label: Sleeping on stomach
scientificBasis: Prone sleeping position may influence subjective dream experiences, possibly associating with themes of restriction or confinement due to altered pressure distribution or subtle respiration changes.

source.author: Yu
source.title: The effect of sleep position on dream experiences

aiInstruction: Present this as a tentative physical-context hypothesis only if the user reports sleeping on their stomach and the dream includes feelings of restriction, being trapped, or breathing load. Do not state as certain cause.
limitations: Relies heavily on self-reported survey data; physiological variables were not directly controlled.

scoring.reason: Sleeping on stomach may cause physical constraint or breathing discomfort.

label: Sleeping on back
scientificBasis: Supine sleeping can be considered a possible contributing factor to a higher frequency of nightmare recall or sleep paralysis symptoms, often correlated with airway vulnerability or transient micro-arousals.
aiInstruction: Frame this as a situational factor when user reports back sleeping accompanied by high-intensity distress. Maintain interpretive neutrality and lower confidence.
limitations: Individual baseline physiological airway mechanics vary significantly. Unverified meta-source summary.

scoring.reason: Sleeping on back is associated with airway vulnerability and micro-arousals.

label: Hot sleeping environment
scientificBasis: High ambient temperature may contribute to reduced sleep quality, leading to increased sleep fragmentation and lighter, more easily recalled vivid REM sleep episodes.

source.author: Sleep and Temperature Literature
source.title: Ambient thermal environments and sleep architecture

aiInstruction: Suggest high room temperature as a potential environmental driver for general sleep discomfort or high dream recall. Avoid mapping heat directly to specific symbols.
limitations: Thermal comfort thresholds are highly subjective across individuals. Citation unverified.

scoring.reason: High ambient temperature fragments sleep and increases vivid nightmares.

label: Cold sleeping environment
scientificBasis: Cold sleeping environments can be considered as a possible factor inducing mild thermal stress during sleep, which may occasionally contribute to general sleep discomfort or bodily cold sensations.

source.author: Environmental Sleep Research
source.title: Cold exposure and REM sleep disruptions

aiInstruction: Treat low room temperature as a possible contextual layer prompting physical cold-sensory themes. Do not directly map this to complex dream symbols or emotional isolation unless explicit user text supports it.
limitations: Evidence maps stronger to sleep architecture disruptions than specific semantic dream imagery. Unverified citation.

scoring.reason: Cold sleeping environment causes mild thermal stress.

label: External environmental noise
scientificBasis: External auditory stimuli present during sleep may occasionally bleed directly into active REM dream narratives, transforming into sensory-integrated elements within the dream scenario.

source.author: Sensory Incorporation Studies
source.title: Auditory stimulus integration during REM sleep

aiInstruction: Use this mechanism to suggest direct external sounds as a possible contributing factor explaining abrupt alarms or loud events inside the dream narrative if the timeline aligns.
limitations: Most intrusive environmental noises trigger sudden complete awakening rather than sustained long-form narrative integration.

scoring.reason: External noise causes sensory disruption and lighter sleep phases.

label: Ambient room light
scientificBasis: Ambient light exposure during late sleep phases may prompt subtle visual leakage or early micro-arousals, occasionally affecting sleep depth parameters.

source.author: Visual Sleep Science
source.title: Photic stimulation and REM sleep parameters

aiInstruction: Mention ambient brightness as a possible factor in sleep fragmentation or micro-arousal. Do not map light to concrete dream imagery elements or clarity scores.
limitations: Eyelid tracking orientation and facial depth heavily alter actual light filtering mechanics.

scoring.reason: Ambient light disrupts circadian rhythm and sleep structure.

label: Heavy eating close to bedtime
scientificBasis: Late heavy meals may increase gastrointestinal metabolism and body temperature, which are parameters sometimes associated with sleep fragmentation and heightened dream vividness.

source.author: Heavy eating studies
source.title: Late heavy meal ingestion and sleep structure

aiInstruction: Frame late eating as a possible contributing factor for lighter sleep and frequent awakenings, which naturally enhances dream recall. Do not infer specific symbolic interpretations from food items.
limitations: The chemical makeup of meals and individual baseline digestion speeds create immense variations.

scoring.reason: Heavy late eating increases metabolism and sleep fragmentation.

label: Pre-sleep screen exposure
scientificBasis: High exposure to blue light before bedtime may delay melatonin synthesis, potentially altering initial sleep architecture and delaying deep sleep phases.

source.author: Circadian Rhythm Research
source.title: Digital screen exposure and melatonin inhibition dynamics

aiInstruction: Link pre-sleep screen use to altered sleep timing and potential REM intensity later in the cycle. Do not build semantic content interpretations based on this.
limitations: Screen brightness settings, hardware filters, and proximity dynamically scale the total light impact.

scoring.reason: Pre-sleep screen time delays melatonin onset.

label: Consuming high-stress media before bed
scientificBasis: Consuming high-stress or horror media directly before sleep may act as an immediate cognitive priming mechanism, increasing the likelihood that immediate threat themes appear in subsequent dream imagery.

source.author: Cognitive Priming Literature
source.title: Media consumption and pre-sleep cognitive arousal

aiInstruction: Present this clearly as a possible immediate cognitive priming factor. Highlight that high-stress media content may provide the structural elements processed in the dream architecture.
limitations: Individual emotional resilience and ongoing media desensitization alter the baseline priming payload.

scoring.reason: Consuming high-stress media cognitive primes the brain for threat scenarios.

label: Acute sleep deprivation
scientificBasis: Accumulating significant acute sleep debt often triggers a physiological REM Rebound effect upon recovery, characterized by highly concentrated, dense, and emotional REM cycles.

source.author: Sleep Architecture Science
source.title: The mechanics of REM sleep rebound cycles

aiInstruction: Explain the unusual intensity and emotional weight of the dream as a possible physiological consequence of a REM Rebound cycle following sleep debt. Avoid complex symbolic readings.
limitations: Chronic sleep deprivation profiles display completely different sleep architecture patterns than acute sleep debt.

scoring.reason: Acute sleep debt leads to intense REM rebound cycles with high distress.

label: Continuity Hypothesis
scientificBasis: The Continuity Hypothesis suggests that dream content may frequently reflect the active waking-life concerns, experiences, relationship matrices, and core emotions of the individual.

source.author: Schredl
source.title: Continuity between waking life and dreaming

aiInstruction: Utilize this established psychological framework to suggest potential links between active dream themes and real waking-life worries or experiences reported by the user. Do not claim absolute certainty.
limitations: Dreams do not always directly or cleanly mirror waking life; highly abstract structural distortions are common.

scoring.reason: Continuity hypothesis is an interpretive framework only.

label: Threat Simulation Theory (TST)
scientificBasis: Threat Simulation Theory posits that an evolutionary function of dreaming may be to simulate dangerous scenarios in a safe environment, allowing the cognitive apparatus to rehearse threat detection and avoidance behaviors.

source.author: Revonsuo
source.title: The reinterpretation of dreams: An evolutionary hypothesis

aiInstruction: Apply this theory to evaluate severe threat or chase sequences in dreams. Frame the experience as a potential evolutionary training scenario designed to practice avoidance, helping lower waking anxiety levels.
limitations: Not all dreams feature threat architectures; low-arousal or peaceful dream profiles do not align with this theory.

scoring.reason: Threat simulation is an interpretive framework only.

label: Memory Consolidation Process
scientificBasis: Neuroscientific data indicates that sleep plays a vital role in day-to-day memory consolidation, causing fragments of recent learning experiences and older memory elements to occasionally mix inside the dream architecture.
aiInstruction: Explain bizarre or highly fragmented scene changes inside the dream as a natural byproduct of the brain sorting, cross-referencing, and stabilizing memory items during sleep cycles.
limitations: The precise mechanism converting synaptic consolidation steps into subjective semantic dream imagery remains unproven.

scoring.reason: Memory consolidation is an interpretive framework only.

label: Emotion Regulation Theory
scientificBasis: The Emotion Regulation model suggests that REM sleep may act as a form of overnight emotional therapy, processing intense emotional experiences to help strip away their acute physiological distress profile.

source.author: Affective Sleep Science
source.title: REM sleep and emotional memory down-regulation

aiInstruction: Interpret intense emotions or stress markers as the mind working to digest active affective weights. Present this as a possible emotional processing mechanism, while noting that repeated distressing dreams may require a more cautious interpretation.
limitations: Severe emotional trauma can override this regulation cycle, resulting in repetitive, unintegrated nightmares instead. Unverified citation.

scoring.reason: Emotion regulation theory is an interpretive framework only.

label: High Neuroticism Profile Alignment
scientificBasis: The personality trait of Neuroticism relates to a measured sensitivity towards negative affect, which may show correlations with higher baseline threat vulnerability appraisals and instances of nightmare distress.

source.author: Five-Factor Personality Model Research
source.title: Personality traits and subjective sleep quality metrics

aiInstruction: Only trigger this rule if the user has a measured high neuroticism profile. Evaluate repetitive fear or emotional reactivity as a reflection of high baseline emotional sensitivity. STRICT RULE: Do not issue clinical or psychological diagnoses.
limitations: A single personality metric is a broad indicator and cannot reliably determine the exact layout of specific daily dreams.

scoring.reason: Profile-based scoring is handled by Component B to avoid double-counting.

label: Cultural Analysis Limitation Guard
scientificBasis: Cultural symbolic typologies like Numerology or Zodiac systems function strictly as subjective interpretive frameworks. They lack empirical predictive validity in modern scientific psychology.

source.author: Anthropological & Epistemological Literature
source.title: The cognitive structure of traditional symbolic frameworks

aiInstruction: Treat all zodiac or numerological data indicators strictly as cultural allegories or thematic lenses requested by the user. You MUST explicitly state that these frameworks function as reflective symbolic tools, not factual, empirical, or deterministic predictions.
limitations: These traditional typologies do not represent objective mental states or causal psychological parameters.

scoring.reason: Cultural symbolic frameworks do not change numeric scores.
